title: Quick check: the ceiling and the two-question test

questions.prompt: A tech finishes the three courses and passes the final scenario assessment with 85%. What does the result actually authorise?
choices: The tech is now signed off to work all client domains unsupervised.
The tech is ready for the MSP's own shadow-and-sign-off process.
The tech now has authority to override the ceiling on items they feel confident about.
The tech can execute DNSSEC operations without sign-off if they're confident.
questions.explanation: The LMS pass is a recommendation of readiness, not the MSP's sign-off. The LMS says the tech can make the calls; the MSP review says the tech makes them well on real customers. The course completion is one input to the second, not a replacement for it.

questions.prompt: The two-question reversibility test asks two things before any change to a live domain or DNS record. What are they?
choices: Is the change urgent? Has the client approved it?
If I do this and it's wrong, is it reversible? Do I understand WHY this is the right action.
Has a senior signed off? Is this documented in the runbook?
Will it cost the client money? Will it affect uptime?
questions.explanation: Or am I copying steps? The two questions, exactly. If either answer is no — escalate. Sign-off and runbooks are part of how the test gets answered for high-blast-radius work, not a substitute for it. Urgency and approval matter operationally but aren't the reflex test the ceiling teaches.
